title: 5 Mulanje Tea Estates Worth Visiting on Your Malawi Trip
meta_title: 5 Mulanje Tea Estates Worth Visiting on Your Malawi Trip
meta_description: Explore the best Mulanje tea estates including Lujeri, Satemwa and Conforzi. Factory tours, Blue Lagoon, tea tastings and where to stay in tea country.
author: The Thyolo House
excerpt: Southern Malawi's tea country stretches from the slopes of Mount Mulanje through the misty Thyolo highlands, covering some of the oldest and most scenic plantations in Africa. Here are five estates worth building a trip around — from factory tours and wild swimming to century-old Italian heritage and some of the best food in the country.
content:
Southern Malawi's tea country is one of those places that nobody tells you about, and then you arrive and wonder how you missed it. The Mulanje tea estates spread across the foothills of Africa's most dramatic inselberg and into the rolling highlands of Thyolo district, covering thousands of hectares in neatly clipped green rows that look, from a distance, like velvet draped over the hills. Tea has been grown here since 1908. It is now the backbone of Malawi's position as the second-largest tea producer in Africa, with over 50,000 seasonal workers employed across the region and tea accounting for roughly 8–9% of the country's export revenue.

Yet for all its agricultural importance, this region barely appears on most travel itineraries. That is exactly what makes it worth visiting. There are no crowds. There are no tour buses. There are just red dirt roads, birdsong, cool highland air, and a landscape layered with more than a century of history. Here are five estates — from Mulanje to Thyolo — that reward the traveller willing to turn off the highway.

Why Malawi's Tea Country Deserves a Spot on Your Itinerary

Most visitors to Malawi head straight for the lake, and the lake is magnificent. But the southern highlands offer something entirely different — a slower, cooler, greener world where agriculture and history have shaped every hill and valley. The tea estates of Mulanje and Thyolo sit between 600 and 1,000 metres above sea level, which means pleasant temperatures year-round, misty mornings, and a lushness that surprises people expecting the dry savanna of southern Africa.

What makes these estates interesting as a travel destination, rather than just a commodity story, is the human layer. The families and companies that planted tea here over the last century brought Scottish, Italian, and British traditions and grafted them onto Malawian soil. The old planters' bungalows, the factory buildings with their industrial Victorian bones, the forest fragments preserved between plantation blocks — all of it creates a landscape that feels both working and almost dreamlike. Add to that the proximity of Mount Mulanje itself, rising to 3,002 metres just to the east, and you have one of the most visually striking agricultural regions anywhere in Africa.

A Mulanje tea estates road trip can be done in a single long day from Blantyre, but two or three nights gives you time to actually settle in, walk the plantations, visit a factory, eat well, and perhaps hike a trail on Mulanje itself. It combines well with Blantyre city breaks, Mulanje day trips, or longer circuits that take in Zomba and Lake Malawi.

1. Lujeri Tea Estate — Factory Tours, Blue Lagoon & a Mountain Lodge

Lujeri is the estate that most travellers encounter first if they are coming from the Mulanje side. Located on the lower slopes of Mount Mulanje itself, Lujeri is Malawi's second-largest tea producer, with extensive plantations climbing from around 600 metres up into the foothills. The estate has been a significant employer in the district for decades, and at its peak processes enormous volumes of leaf during the rainy season between December and May.

What to See and Do

Lujeri's biggest draw for visitors is the combination of tea and landscape. The estate's proximity to Mount Mulanje means the scenery is spectacular — sheer granite cliffs rising behind manicured tea rows. Factory tours are available and give a good introduction to the withering, rolling, oxidation, and drying process that transforms fresh leaf into the black tea that ends up in cups across the world. Advance booking is recommended.

The Blue Lagoon, a natural pool fed by streams running off Mulanje, is a popular side trip within the estate area. It is a beautiful spot for a swim after a morning of walking, though access conditions can vary — ask locally about current accessibility.

Lujeri also operates a guesthouse that has historically offered basic but comfortable accommodation for visitors exploring the Mulanje area. For those planning to combine a tea visit with hiking on Mulanje, the estate is well positioned as a base.

Practical Notes

It is worth noting that Lujeri suspended production in August 2025 due to labour disputes, and its operational status for 2026 visitors may vary. Check locally or ask your accommodation to confirm tour availability before making it the centrepiece of your itinerary. Even if the factory is quiet, the landscape and Blue Lagoon are still worth the drive, and the estate roads make for excellent walking.

  • Location: Lower slopes of Mount Mulanje, approximately 30 minutes from Mulanje town
  • Best for: Combining tea with Mulanje hiking, Blue Lagoon swimming
  • Season note: Peak picking activity December–May

2. Conforzi Tea Estate — A Century-Old Italian Legacy in Thyolo

Drive west from Mulanje into the Thyolo highlands and the landscape softens — the jagged granite of Mulanje gives way to rounded, tea-covered hills and patches of indigenous forest. This is where the Conforzi family, Italian immigrants who arrived in the early 1900s, built a tea estate that has been in continuous family ownership for over a century.

The Conforzi estate occupies a unique place in Malawi's tea story. While most of the region's plantations were founded by British or Scottish settlers, the Conforzi family brought an Italian sensibility to the highlands — a feel for food, art, and the rhythms of a Mediterranean farming life transplanted to African soil. You can read the full history in our piece on the story of Conforzi Tea Estate.

The Thyolo House — Where the Estate Comes to Life

Today, the Conforzi estate is home to The Thyolo House, a boutique hotel and restaurant created by Flavia Conforzi, a fourth-generation Italian-Malawian artist. Flavia has transformed the family's historic farmhouse into a five-room retreat that feels like stepping into someone's beautifully curated private world — because that is exactly what it is. Every room is different, shaped by Flavia's hand-painted murals, collected art, and an eye for beauty that turns ordinary spaces into something memorable.

But it is the integration with the estate that sets this apart. You can walk from your room directly onto tea plantation trails, through indigenous forest alive with rare birds (including the endangered Thyolo Alethe), past the kitchen garden where the restaurant sources its herbs and vegetables, and back in time for lunch. The boutique rooms sit within historic structures on the estate, renovated to preserve original features while adding real comfort — no roughing it required.

The Restaurant

The restaurant at The Thyolo House is reason enough to make the drive. Flavia runs an Italian-fusion kitchen that draws on ingredients grown in the estate's own garden — hand-rolled pasta, wood-fired dishes, salads that were picked that morning. For a region where dining options are limited, the quality here is genuinely exceptional. Many visitors staying elsewhere in the area drive to The Thyolo House specifically for dinner.

Practical Notes

  • Location: Thyolo district, 20 minutes from Limbe, 40 minutes from Blantyre
  • Rooms: 5 individually designed suites
  • Activities: Tea plantation walks, forest trails, art workshops, pool, garden-to-table dining
  • Best for: Travellers who want to sleep, eat, and explore within a working tea estate
  • Contact: WhatsApp +265 884 202 040 or thethyolohouse@gmail.com

3. Satemwa Tea Estate — Tastings, Heritage Trails & Huntingdon House

Satemwa is the most established visitor-facing estate in the region and the one most likely to appear in guidebooks. Founded in 1923 by Scottish immigrant Maclean Kay — a former rubber planter from Malaya who saw the potential in Thyolo's misty highlands — it remains family-owned by the Cathcart Kay family, now in its fourth generation. It is one of the oldest continuously family-owned tea estates in Africa.

What to See and Do

Satemwa has invested more than any other estate in the visitor experience. The headline offering is the tea tasting, which costs around USD $10 per person and features up to 20 varieties — from robust breakfast blends (Satemwa supplies PG Tips, Lipton, Tetley's, and 5 Roses) to delicate white teas and hand-rolled specialty lots. The tasting is led by the estate's resident tea expert and is surprisingly engaging, even for people who think they do not care about tea.

Factory tours take you through the 1937-era processing building, where you watch fresh leaf go through withering, rolling, oxidation, drying, and sorting — all within hours of being picked. It is a sensory experience: the warmth, the earthy aroma, the rhythmic clatter of the rolling machines.

Beyond the factory, Satemwa offers over 100 miles of dirt roads and paths for mountain biking (bikes available from Huntingdon House), seasonal coffee tours through wild coffee fields, birdwatching in forest patches, and hiking on Thyolo Mountain. For a deeper look at what the area offers, see our guide to Thyolo tea estate tours.

Where to Stay

Satemwa's Huntingdon House is a colonial-era lodge in the founder's former home, offering five ensuite rooms, three-course dinners with estate-grown ingredients, and views across to Mount Mulanje and the Shire River. It is a luxury-tier experience — specialist tour operators typically price packages at USD $250–500 per person per night. Booking well in advance is essential, as it operates at small scale.

For groups, the Chawani Bungalow on Thyolo Mountain offers a four-bedroom self-catering house (up to nine guests) at approximately USD $150–175 per night.

Practical Notes

  • Location: Thyolo district, approximately 50 km southwest of Blantyre
  • Advance booking required for all tours and activities — walk-ins are not accommodated
  • Contact: +265 (0) 997 725 777
  • Best for: Dedicated tea enthusiasts, mountain biking, luxury stays

4. Naming'omba Tea Estate — A Quiet Corner of Thyolo's Plantation Belt

Not every estate needs a headline attraction. Naming'omba sits in the quieter southern reaches of Thyolo district, part of the extensive network of plantations that have shaped this landscape for over a century. It is less geared toward visitors than Satemwa or The Thyolo House, but for travellers who want to see working tea country without the filter of a polished tourist experience, it offers something authentic.

What to Expect

Naming'omba is primarily a working estate, and the appeal is the landscape itself — the endless green geometry of the tea rows, the workers moving through the fields during picking season, the views west toward the Lower Shire Valley. Driving through the estate roads gives you a sense of the sheer scale of Malawi's tea industry that you do not get from a single tasting room.

There are no formal visitor facilities in the way that Satemwa provides, but the estate roads are open for driving and walking, and the surrounding area includes villages and markets that give a glimpse of daily life in rural Thyolo. If you are staying at The Thyolo House or elsewhere in the district, Naming'omba makes for a pleasant half-day drive that loops through some of the most scenic tea country in the region.

Practical Notes

  • Location: Southern Thyolo district
  • No formal tour booking — this is a drive-through-and-explore experience
  • Best for: Photography, seeing working plantations, off-the-beaten-track driving
  • Combine with: A loop drive through Thyolo's plantation belt, returning to your accommodation for dinner

5. Eastern Produce Estates — The Industrial Backbone of Mulanje Tea

Eastern Produce Malawi (EPM) is the elephant in the room — or rather, across the room, the valley, and several hills. As the largest tea producer in Malawi, responsible for approximately 38% of the country's tea exports, EPM operates a constellation of estates across both Mulanje and Thyolo districts. Names like Esperanza, Lauderdale, Mini Mini, Ruo, Glenorchy, Likanga, Eldorado, and Thornwood dot the map of the region.

Why It Matters

You cannot understand the Mulanje tea estates landscape without understanding EPM. The company's plantations sit at elevations between 600 and 800 metres, and its operations employ thousands of workers during the growing season. This is industrial-scale tea production — the kind that fills shipping containers bound for blending houses in Mombasa, London, and Dubai. It is the economic engine that keeps the entire district running.

Visiting EPM Estates

EPM estates are not set up for casual tourism in the way that Satemwa is. There are no public tasting rooms or bookable factory tours. However, for travellers with a genuine interest in the tea industry — journalists, agricultural tourists, or anyone writing about Malawi's economy — it is sometimes possible to arrange visits through the company's offices in Mulanje or Thyolo. This requires advance planning and a clear purpose for your visit.

Even without a formal tour, driving through EPM estates gives you a powerful sense of scale. The plantations stretch for kilometres in every direction, and during the peak picking season (December–March) you will see lines of workers moving through the fields in a way that has not fundamentally changed in a century. The factory buildings, some of them dating back decades, are impressive pieces of industrial architecture in their own right.

Industry Context

The tea industry in this region is not without its challenges. Worker compensation disputes have made headlines in recent years, and climate change is increasingly affecting yields and plant health. The Tea Research Foundation, based in Mulanje, is experimenting with high-yield clones and climate-resilient cultivars to safeguard the industry's future. Malawi's production target of approximately 51,000 metric tons by 2026 reflects both the ambition and the pressure facing the sector.

  • Location: Multiple estates across Mulanje and Thyolo districts
  • Visitor access: By arrangement only — not a standard tourist activity
  • Best for: Understanding the industry at scale, agricultural tourism, photography from estate roads

How to Plan a Mulanje Tea Estates Road Trip (Routes, Distances & Best Season)

A tea estates road trip works best as a loop starting and ending in Blantyre, Malawi's commercial capital. Here is how to structure it.

The Route

From Blantyre, head southeast on the M2 toward Mulanje (approximately 70 km, 1.5–2 hours depending on road conditions). Visit Lujeri and the Blue Lagoon area on the slopes of Mount Mulanje. Then drive west through the tea country toward Thyolo district (about 45 minutes), stopping at EPM estates along the way. Base yourself in Thyolo for one or two nights — at The Thyolo House on the Conforzi Estate or at Satemwa's Huntingdon House — and use Thyolo as a base to visit Satemwa and explore the surrounding plantation belt, including Naming'omba. The return to Blantyre from Thyolo is a quick 40-minute drive.

Suggested Itineraries

One-day trip: Blantyre → Mulanje (Lujeri, Blue Lagoon) → Thyolo (lunch at The Thyolo House, quick Satemwa visit) → Blantyre. Doable but rushed — you will be driving more than exploring.

Two nights (recommended): Day 1: Blantyre → Mulanje area, explore Lujeri and Mount Mulanje foothills. Drive to Thyolo, check into The Thyolo House, dinner at the restaurant. Day 2: Morning tea plantation walk and forest trail on the Conforzi Estate, afternoon Satemwa tasting and factory tour. Day 3: Leisurely morning, drive back to Blantyre via Limbe.

Three nights (the full experience): Add a day for a day trip to Mount Mulanje — the Likabula Forest trail or a hike to the Lichenya Plateau — plus time for art workshops, mountain biking at Satemwa, or simply sitting by the pool with a book.

Best Season

  • May–October (dry season): Clear skies, comfortable temperatures (15–22°C), best road conditions. Tea bushes are less actively growing but factories still operate and the landscape is green.
  • December–March (peak picking): The most visually dramatic time — workers moving through bright green rows, factories at full capacity, the hills at their most lush. Afternoon rain showers are common and dirt roads can be muddy.
  • November and April: Shoulder months. Good green coverage, fewer visitors, occasional rain. A solid compromise.

Getting Around

You will need a private vehicle for a tea estates road trip. The main highways are tarmac and generally well maintained, but estate roads and some connecting routes are graded dirt — passable in a standard car during the dry season but ideally suited to a 4x4 or high-clearance vehicle, especially during the rains. Hiring a driver from Blantyre is straightforward and recommended if you want to enjoy the scenery without navigating unfamiliar roads.

Where to Stay Between Tea Estate Visits

Accommodation options in tea country are limited but characterful. Here are your best choices.

The Thyolo House (Conforzi Estate)

Five individually designed boutique rooms in historic estate buildings, an Italian-fusion restaurant that is the best dining for miles, a swimming pool, direct access to tea plantations and forest trails, and the personal warmth of Flavia Conforzi's hospitality. This is where we recommend basing yourself for a Thyolo stay — it is the most complete visitor experience in the area, combining comfort, culture, food, and landscape in a way that is hard to find anywhere else in Malawi. The Thyolo House is 40 minutes from Blantyre, 20 minutes from Limbe, and roughly four hours from Lake Malawi, making it a natural stopover on longer southern Malawi circuits.

Huntingdon House (Satemwa Estate)

The colonial-era luxury option. Five rooms in the 1928 family home, with three-course dinners and estate views. Expect to pay USD $250–500 per person per night through specialist operators. Book well in advance — it fills quickly, especially in the dry season.

Mulanje Town

If you are focusing on the Mulanje side and plan to hike, basic guesthouses and lodges are available in Mulanje town and near the Likabula Forest entrance. Standards are modest — this is functional accommodation rather than a destination stay. For something more comfortable, it is worth driving the 45 minutes to Thyolo and basing there.

Blantyre Day Base

If you prefer to sleep in a city and day-trip, Blantyre has a range of hotels at all price points. The drive to Thyolo is 40 minutes and to Mulanje about 1.5 hours, so day trips are feasible — but you miss the magic of early mornings and late evenings on the estates, when the light is at its most beautiful and the hills are at their quietest.

Ready to Plan Your Visit?

If you are thinking about a Mulanje tea estates trip and want help shaping an itinerary — or simply want to book a room and a table — message us on WhatsApp. We know this region inside out and are happy to help you plan, whether you have one night or a week. You can also email us at thethyolohouse@gmail.com.

Malawi's tea country does not advertise itself. There are no billboards, no glossy visitor centres, no queues. It is a place you have to seek out, and that is precisely what makes it worth the drive. Come for the tea, stay for the landscape, and eat very well in between.
